ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai holy basil chicken rice (Pad Krapao Gai). The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03311737). A more specific term for this composite Thai stir-fry dish is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for the chicken component of this composite Thai stir-fry rice dish; no composite FoodEx2 code for Pad Krapao Gai exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific composite code for Thai holy basil chicken rice. A0CKM (cooked chicken meat) is used as the primary protein anchor. The rice component maps to A00LC (cooked rice) but a single composite code is unavailable.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Pad Krapao Gai or Thai holy basil chicken rice as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Kra Pao Basil Chicken Rice as typically served in a Thai restaurant or hawker stall in Singapore weighs approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g steamed jasmine rice and 150 g of the holy basil chicken stir-fry topping.

nutrient_source.title: Kra Pao Basil Chicken Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kra Pao Basil Chicken Rice (composite dish, per 100 g). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95 as indicated in the input per_100g_meta. Saturated fat is estimated from the HPB fat value using standard Thai stir-fry fat composition ratios (approximately 25% saturated of total fat), as the HPB record does not disaggregate fat fractions; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for jasmine rice (cooked)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite Kra Pao Basil Chicken Rice dish. GI is estimated at 69 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked jasmine (white) rice, which has a published GI of approximately 68–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The stir-fry topping (chicken, basil, sauces) contributes negligible additional carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI of the composite dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (69 × 15.12 × 3.5) / 100 = 69 × 52.92 / 100 ≈ 36.5 for the full 350 g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 324 mg/100 g with insufficient potassium (182 mg/100 g) and fibre (1.0 g/100 g) offset; a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,134 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce when ordering, avoid adding table condiments, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Steamed jasmine rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with a high GI (~69) and the full 350 g serving delivers approximately 53 g of carbohydrate, creating a high glycaemic load; dietary fibre at 1.0 g/100 g provides minimal glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request brown rice or reduce the rice portion by half, monitor total carbohydrate per serving carefully, and be aware that fish sauce and oyster sauce contribute additional sugar to the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 324 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; total fat is moderate at 4.0 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat of ~1.0 g/100 g, and cholesterol is low at 13 mg/100 g; however, the high sodium load per full serving poses a meaningful blood-pressure-mediated cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request sauce reduction to lower sodium intake, and note that the lean chicken protein and anti-inflammatory holy basil are favourable components when consumed in a reduced-sodium preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Holy basil (kra pao) is rich in eugenol and rosmarinic acid with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties; chicken is a lean white meat with low arachidonic acid burden; saturated fat is low at approximately 1.0 g/100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory polyphenols in holy basil and garlic, but should request reduced fish sauce and oyster sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, and monitor individual tolerance to chilli as a nightshade vegetable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kra Pao Basil Chicken Rice (Thai: ข้าวผัดกะเพราไก่, romanized: Khao Pad Krapao Gai) is a classic Thai stir-fry dish consisting of minced or sliced chicken rapidly wok-fried with fresh holy basil (kra pao / Ocimum tenuiflorum), garlic, and fresh bird's-eye chillies, seasoned with fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, then served over a bed of steamed jasmine rice. The dish is a staple of Thai street food and is widely available in Thai restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, spicy, and aromatic, with the distinctive anise-clove fragrance of holy basil dominating the palate. The texture contrast between the tender, juicy chicken and the soft jasmine rice is characteristic of the dish. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 15.12 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 324 mg/100 g representing the primary clinical concern. The high-GI jasmine rice base (GI ~69) and moderate carbohydrate content make glycaemic management important for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The sodium load from multiple high-sodium condiments (fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce) makes this dish a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Holy basil contributes polyphenolic anti-inflammatory compounds (eugenol, rosmarinic acid) that may be beneficial for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is dairy-free and contains common allergens including fish (fish sauce), shellfish (oyster sauce), soy, and gluten.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a stated confidence of 0.95. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. The dish identity is unambiguous. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the dominant carbohydrate component (jasmine rice), which is a defensible and documented analogous estimate. Saturated fat is estimated from total fat using standard Thai stir-fry composition ratios and is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 1.0 g/100 g (approximately 25% of total fat 4.0 g/100 g) based on typical Thai stir-fry fat composition using vegetable oil; the HPB Foodo record does not disaggregate fat fractions. This is an approximation and should be updated if HPB publishes disaggregated fat data. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite Thai stir-fry rice dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central does not contain an exact match for this composite dish. 3. GI of 69 is an analogous estimate based on cooked jasmine rice (GI 68–72, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving is calculated for a 350 g standard plate: GL = (69 × 15.12 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 36.5. 4. Halal status is listed as false because oyster sauce may contain non-halal shellfish derivatives and the dish preparation context is not certified halal; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. 5. The dish name in the input record includes a restaurant prefix 'R3' which has been removed from the canonical food_name to produce a clean, reusable food identity. The original menu name is preserved in aliases.
